coredata.dc:title: Characterization of carbon quantum dots doped with various heteroatoms and their influence on inhibiting the browning of fresh-cut potatoes

coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Yefei
Zhang, Xinhua
Zhang, Yanfang
Shang, Jin
Lv, Meng
Li, Xiaoan
Li, Fujun
coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to explore how various heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) influence the browning of fruits and vegetables. Five different CQDs were synthesized with the heteroatom nitrogen, selenium, calcium, potassium, and boron, respectively. Their structures were separately characterized using transmission electron microscope, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Then, their in vitro antioxidant capacities were assessed, and the heteroatom-doped CQDs that exhibited the strongest antioxidant capacities was used to determine the optimal concentrations for preventing browning in freshly cut potatoes. The findings revealed that calcium-doped CQDs (Ca-CQDs) exhibited the highest antioxidant capacity in vitro. At a concentration of 1.00 mg/mL, Ca-CQDs significantly inhibited the activities of potato polyphenol oxidase, peroxidase, and tyrosinase, lowered malondialdehyde levels, and enhanced the activity of phenylalanine dismutase, total phenol content, and free radical scavenging ability of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl and 2,2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid), effectively reducing browning in potatoes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Heteroatom doping
Calcium
Potato
Browning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Screening effective concentration of Ca-CQDs to prevent browning in fresh-cut potatoes
Assessment of the browning inhibition effect of Ca-CQDs on freshly sliced potatoes
Measurement of total phenol and malondialdehyde (MDA)
UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of heteroatom-doped CQDs
Comparison of antioxidant capacity of heteroatom-doped CQDs
Effect of Ca-CQDs treatments on browning of fresh-cut potatoes during storage period
Effect of Ca-CQDs on browning-related enzymes, total phenol, MDA content and antioxidant capacity of fresh-cut potatoes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM mapping of heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots. A to F are the TEM maps of B-CQDs, Se-CQDs, N-CQDs, CQDs, Ca-CQDs, and K-CQDs, respectively.
Fourier infrared spectra of heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots.
UV absorption spectra of heteroatom-doped CQDs (A) and digital photographs of heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots in daylight (B) and under UV irradiation (C).

XPS energy spectrum of heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots. A to F are the full spectra of N-CQDs, K-CQDs, B-CQDs, Se-CQDs, Ca-CQDs and CQDs, respectively; G is the high-resolution C1s spectra of the heteroatom-doped CQDs; H is the high-resolution O1s spectra of the heteroatom-doped CQDs; and I is the high-resolution spectra of the doped elements. J and K are the high-resolution C1s and O1s spectra of CQDs, respectively.

Radical scavenging rates of ABTS (A) and DPPH (B) of carbon quantum dots doped with different heteroatoms. Different lower-case letters at the same treatment concentration indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

Effect of different concentrations of Ca-CQDs (A) and different Ca treatments (B) on the degree of browning of fresh-cut potatoes during cold storage. The fresh-cut potato slices were packaged with 0.05 mm thick polyethylene film and stored at 4–8 °C. Different lowercase letters indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) within different concentrations (A) and different Ca treatments (B).

Effects of different treatments on browning-related enzymes, total phenols, malondialdehyde content and antioxidant capacity of fresh-cut potatoes. The fresh-cut potato slices were packaged with 0.05 mm thick polyethylene film and stored at 4–8 °C. Different lower-case letters at the same storage time indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).
Elemental content of heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Characterization of carbon quantum dots doped with various heteroatoms and their influence on inhibiting the browning of fresh-cut potatoes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: No. 266 West Xincun Road, Zhangdian District, Zibo City, Shandong Province, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No. 266 West Xincun Road, Zhangdian District

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to explore how various heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) influence the browning of fruits and vegetables. Five different CQDs were synthesized with the heteroatom nitrogen, selenium, calcium, potassium, and boron, respectively. Their structures were separately characterized using transmission electron microscope, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Then, their in vitro antioxidant capacities were assessed, and the heteroatom-doped CQDs that exhibited the strongest antioxidant capacities was used to determine the optimal concentrations for preventing browning in freshly cut potatoes. The findings revealed that calcium-doped CQDs (Ca-CQDs) exhibited the highest antioxidant capacity in vitro. At a concentration of 1.00 mg/mL, Ca-CQDs significantly inhibited the activities of potato polyphenol oxidase, peroxidase, and tyrosinase, lowered malondialdehyde levels, and enhanced the activity of phenylalanine dismutase, total phenol content, and free radical scavenging ability of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl and 2,2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid), effectively reducing browning in potatoes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanosized carbon materials made up primarily of carbon atoms, with sizes generally ranging from 1 to 10 nm, which is why they are referred to as 'quantum dots' (Ezati, Priyadarshi et al., 2022). Research has shown that CQDs exhibit unique optical properties, exceptional antimicrobial activity, and remarkable antioxidant capabilities. Additionally, their biocompatibility and bioactivity render them safe for human use. Consequently, CQDs are considered innovative materials suitable for applications in food safety preservation and inspection, food packaging, as well as in fertilization and catalysis within energy production (Wang et al., 2017; Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023; Yao et al., 2024). Recently, it has been observed that CQDs, whether used alone (Manikandan & Min, 2023) or in conjunction with other substances like chitosan (Riahi et al., 2022; Wang, Qui et al., 2023), can serve as antioxidants to decelerate the oxidation of fruits and vegetables, while also inhibiting the growth of bacteria and fungi, thereby extending their freshness.

Heteroatom doping of CQDs involves incorporating atoms from different elements, like nitrogen (N), phosphorus, and sulfur, into the CQD structure. Kou et al. (2020) and Kong et al. (2024) have pointed out that the doping of N, boron (B) and selenium (Se) atoms differentially altered the electrical, optical and antioxidant properties of CQDs, thereby broadening their application areas. Miao et al. (2020) reported that N-doped CQDs improved the antioxidant properties and photocatalytic activity of CQDs, which aided in delaying oxidation and inhibiting bacterial growth. In addition, both potassium (K) and calcium (Ca) play crucial roles in the excellent quality formation of fruits storage (Bai et al., 2021). Wang, Liu et al. (2023) demonstrated that the application of Ca doped CQDs nanofertilizers significantly enhanced the Ca concentration in apple fruits.

Fresh-cut fruits and vegetables are a new product model that has rapidly thrived and developed in recent years to meet diverse consumer demands (Chen et al., 2024). Browning poses a significant challenge, affecting the color, nutritional value, and overall quality of these fresh-cut fruits and vegetables. This process involves a complex enzymatic reaction among various enzymes, including polyphenol oxidase (PPO), tyrosinase (TYR), phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL), and peroxidase (POD), as well as their interactions with compounds like polyphenols (Bouabid et al., 2020). Consequently, strategies to inhibit the activities of browning-related enzymes and enhance their antioxidant capacities of fruits and vegetables have emerged as effective methods to combat enzymatic browning (Murata, 2022).

Therefore, considering the potential application effects of heteroatom-doped CQDs in maintaining fresh-cut fruits and vegetables, this study first prepared five different heteroatom-doped CQDs with N (N-CQDs), Se (Se-CQDs), Ca (Ca-CQDs), K (K-CQDs), and B (B-CQDs). Then, by utilizing transmission electron microscope (SEM), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), ultraviolet spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques to confirm their successful doping, the study selected the CQDs with the best antioxidant effect and their optimal concentration through in vitro antioxidant experiments. Finally, using fresh-cut potatoes prone to enzymatic browning as the test material, the study investigated the effects of heteroatom-doped CQDs immersion treatment on the browning degree, browning-related enzyme activities, membrane lipid peroxidation level, and antioxidant capacity during the storage of fresh-cut potatoes. The primary objective of this study is to provide references for the development of green novel preservatives and their application in controlling browning in fruit and vegetables.

The preparation method was adapted from Chaudhary et al. (2020) with minor modifications. The sources of N, Se, Ca, K, and B included two parts each of urea, selenomethionine, calcium lactate, potassium acetate, and borax, respectively, along with an equal volume of deionized water as a control. Additionally, five parts of citric acid served as the carbon source. After mixing these ingredients, the reaction mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 15 min, followed by microwave heating at 700 W for 5 min. Once cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane and dialyzed in ultrapure water for 48 h with a 1000 Da cutoff. The resulting solution was freeze-dried at −80 °C and stored at 4 °C, and diluted to the desired concentration prior to use.

The UV absorption spectra of the heteroatom-doped CQDs were analyzed using a UV spectrophotometer (UV-1900I, Shimadzu Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The morphology and the size were examined with a TEM (Tecnai G2F 20, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA). The surface chemical bonds and the CC bonds in the CQDs’ core were ascertained with a FTIR (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA). The fluorescence spectra were examined with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, SHIMADZU, Kyoto, Japan), and the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB250XI, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA).

DPPH radical scavenging rate was measured as described by Plazas et al. (2013), with slightly modifications. A 2 mL sample solution was mixed with 2 mL of a 0.04 mg/mL DPPH solution and allowed to react in the dark for 30 min before measuring the absorbance at 518 nm. A 1 mg/mL vitamin C (Vc) standard solution served as the positive control.

ABTS radical scavenging rate was measured as described by Chen et al. (2022), with slightly modifications. A 0.2 mL sample was combined with 3.8 mL of a 0.038 mg/mL ABTS solution and allowed to react in the dark for 10 min before measuring the absorbance at 734 nm. Again, a 1 mg/mL Vc standard solution was used as the positive control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening effective concentration of Ca-CQDs to prevent browning in fresh-cut potatoes
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh, intact potatoes were selected, washed, peeled, and sliced into approximately 0.5 cm thick and 20 g weight pieces with uniform shape and size. These slices were soaked in Ca-CQDs solutions at varying concentrations (0, 0.25, 0.75, 1.00, 1.25 mg/mL) for 15 min, then quickly drained. The slices were sealed in 0.05 mm thick polyethylene film and stored at 4–8 °C. The degree of browning for each treatment was assessed after 8 d of storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the browning inhibition effect of Ca-CQDs on freshly sliced potatoes

ce:section.ce:para: After treatment, the surface moisture was air-dried at room temperature, then sealed and wrapped in 0.05 mm thick polyethylene film, and stored in a refrigerator at 4–8 °C for 8 d, with relevant measurements taken every 2 d.

The color of the fresh-cut potatoes was measured using a colorimeter (HP-2136, Suzhou H&S Optical Technology Co., Ltd., Suzhou, China) following the method of Liu et al. (2018). The L, a, and b values were then used in the equation from Badin et al. (2020): BI= 100 × (x-0.31))/0.172, where x = (a+ 1.75 L)/(5.645 L+a-3.012b), to calculate the BI value.

A 1 g sample of potato was ground and homogenized in 5 mL of pre-cooled phosphate buffer, then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was collected as the enzyme crude extracts for PPO, POD, and TYR. For PAL enzyme extracts, boric acid buffer was used instead of phosphate buffer.

PPO activity was measured using a modified method based on Serradell et al. (2000). The reaction mixture included 4.0 mL of acetate buffer (50 mmol/L, pH 5.5), 1 mL of catechol solution (50 mmol/L), and 0.1 mL of crude enzyme solution. One unit (U) of PPO activity was defined as a 0.01 change in absorbance per minute at 420 nm per gram of potato sample.

POD activity was assessed with slight modifications based on the method of Wang et al. (2009). The reaction mixture contained 3.0 mL of guaiacol solution (25 mmol/L), 0.5 mL of crude enzyme solution, and 0.2 mL of hydrogen peroxide solution (0.5 mol/L). One unit (U) of POD activity was defined as a 0.01 change in absorbance per minute at 470 nm per gram of potato sample.

PAL activity was assessed following the method outlined by Bunsiri et al. (2015). The reaction mixture included 3 mL of a 50 mmol/L boric acid buffer, 0.5 mL of a 20 mmol/L L-phenylalanine solution, and 0.5 mL of crude enzyme solution. One unit (U) of PAL activity was defined as a change in absorbance of 0.01 per minute at 290 nm per gram of potato sample.

TYR activity was measured using the method described by Chai et al. (2019). The reaction mixture consisted of 1 mL of L-dopa (6 mmol/L), 1.5 mL of phosphate buffer, and 0.5 mL of crude enzyme solution. One unit (U) of TYR activity was defined as a 0.01 change in absorbance per minute at 475 nm per gram of potato sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of total phenol and malondialdehyde (MDA)
ce:section.ce:para: The total phenol content was measured according to the method described by Guo et al. (2012). One gram of potato was homogenized in 5 mL of 60 % ethanol in an ice bath. The reaction mixture included 0.25 mL of enzyme extract (supernatant), 2 mL of distilled water, 0.8 mL of 20 % Na2CO3 solution, and 0.25 mL of Folin-phenol reagent. The absorbance at 760 nm was measured, and the total phenol content was expressed as mg of gallic acid equivalent per gram of fresh potato.

MDA content was determined based on the method by Luo et al. (2012). One gram of potato was ground and homogenized in 5.0 mL of a 100 g/L trichloroacetic acid solution. The reaction mixture contained 2.0 mL of supernatant and 2.0 mL of 0.67 % thiobarbituric acid solution. Absorbance values were measured at 450 nm, 532 nm, and 600 nm, and MDA content was reported as μmol/kg.

Data processing involved repeating all experiments at least three times, plotting the results using Origin 9.0 software (Origin Lab, Northampton, USA), and analyzing significant differences (p < 0.05) using One-Way ANOVA and Duncan's Multiple Comparison Procedure in SPSS 21.0.

Results from TEM analysis indicated that the heteroatom-doped CQDs have good monodispersity, with average particle size of 2.6 nm (B-CQDs, Fig. 1A), 2.8 nm (Se-CQDs, Fig. 1B), 3.3 nm (N-CQDs, Fig. 1C), 2.3 nm (CQDs, Fig. 1D), 1.7 nm (Ca-CQDs, Fig. 1E), and 1.2 nm (K-CQDs, Fig. 1F). It is evident that all these sizes are consistent with the characteristics of CQDs, and the incorporating atoms from different chemical elements influence the CQDs' size.

The variation in the chemical bonds on the surface of CQDs after their doping and the CC bonds in the CQDs’ core were evaluated by FTIR spectra ( Fig. 2). FTIR spectra of B-CQDs show that the peaks at 3221, 1720, and 1198 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of O-H, the CO, and the C-O bond, respectively. Additionally, the peak at 819 cm−1 is associated with the deformation vibration of C-H bond, while the peak at 546 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of B-O bond.

FTIR spectra of Se-CQDs exhibit peaks at 3399, 2990, and 1732 cm⁻¹ , which correspond to the stretching vibrations of O-H, C-H, and CO bonds, respectively. Additionally, the peak at 1592 cm⁻¹ is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CC bond, while the peak at 669.43 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the deformation vibration of the C-H bond.

For N-CQDs, the peaks observed at 1563 cm⁻¹ and 1401 cm⁻¹ are associated with the stretching vibrations of N-H and C-N bonds, respectively. In the case of K-CQDs, the peaks at 2975, 1714, and 1580 cm⁻¹ correspond to the stretching vibrations of C-H, CO, and CC bonds, respectively. The peak at 1032 cm⁻¹ indicates the stretching vibration of the C-O bond, suggesting the presence of ether (-O-) or alcohol (-OH) groups within the samples.

For Ca-CQDs, the peaks at 3424, 2988, and 1723 cm⁻¹ correspond to the stretching vibrations of O-H, C-H, and CO bonds, respectively, while the peak at 612 cm⁻¹ is associated with the deformation vibration of the C-C bond. Lastly, for CQDs, the peaks at 3102, 2625, and 1711 correspond to the stretching vibration of O-H, C-H, and CO bond, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 730, 627 and 592 cm−1 are attributed to the deformation vibration of C-H bond.
Therefore，the FTIR spectra confirm the presence of sp2-carbonized structures in the CQDs' cores and oxygen-containing groups at the CQDs' surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of heteroatom-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs typically exhibit strong absorption in the UV range of 210–360 nm, with their absorption bands extending into the visible spectrum (Khan et al., 2022). As illustrated in Fig. 1A, the UV absorption peaks of the CQDs synthesized in this research are primarily found between 280 and 340 nm, aligning with the expected UV absorption properties of CQDs. Two kinds of the characteristic absorption peaks could be recognized in the UV spectra of CQDs and heteroatoms-doped CQDs. An absorption peak at about 290 nm can be attributed to the π-π* leaps of the CC bonds, whereas that at about 340 nm can be ascribed to the π-π* transitions of the CO bonds in the carbonyl groups (Kostromin et al., 2023). Notably, the N-CQDs solution has a peak UV absorption wavelength of 322 nm, appearing light yellow under visible light and exhibiting blue fluorescence under UV light, with fluorescence intensity significantly surpassing that of CQDs doped with K, Se, Ca, and B. This enhanced fluorescence may result from N doping, which introduces numerous reactive groups (-OH, -COOH, -CN, -NH2) that increased surface state defects, thereby improving the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity of the CQDs, leading to higher fluorescence quantum yield and electron concentration (Syed et al., 2022). The B-CQDs solution shows a maximum UV absorption wavelength of 278 nm, appears colorless in visible light, and emits weak blue fluorescence under UV light. The reduced fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs may be attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions among the B-OH groups on the surface, which can cause CQD aggregation and result in fluorescence quenching (Kou et al., 2020). The Se-CQDs solution has a peak UV absorption wavelength of 324 nm, appears brownish-yellow in visible light, and emits blue-green fluorescence under UV light. Se-CQDs exhibit broad absorption in the UV–visible spectrum, consistent with the findings reported by Luo et al. (2020). The Ca-CQDs solution has a maximum UV absorption wavelength of 288 nm, is colorless in visible light, and emits blue-green fluorescence when exposed to UV light. Similarly, the K-CQDs solution has a peak UV absorption wavelength of 292 nm, appears colorless in visible light, and emits blue fluorescence under UV light. Both Ca-CQDs and K-CQDs, being metal ion-doped CQDs, exhibit strong and broad absorption bands in the 200–400 nm range, in line with the research by Chen et al. (2016).

To better understand the optical characteristics of heteroatom-doped CQDs, we analyzed their fluorescence spectra across excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 360 nm, in increments of 10 nm. The results showed that altering the excitation wavelength significantly affected both the fluorescence emission peaks and the intensity of the synthesized CQDs: with the excitation wavelength increased from 290 nm to 360 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks shifted to longer wavelengths (red-shifted), while the fluorescence intensity initially rose and then fell (Fig. 4). Notably, for excitation wavelengths below 300 nm, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, Ca-CQDs, and B-CQDs increased; however, for wavelengths above 300 nm, the intensity began to decline gradually. Consequently, 300 nm was identified as the optimal excitation wavelength for N-CQDs, Ca-CQDs, and B-CQDs, resulting in emission wavelengths of 415, 408, and 404 nm, respectively. For K-CQDs and Se-CQDs, the maximum excitation wavelength was found to be 310 nm, with corresponding emission wavelengths of 400 nm and 401 nm. Furthermore, all heteroatom-doped CQDs emitted light in the blue region (Fig. 4), as evidenced by their blue fluorescence under UV lamp exposure (
Fig. 3). This observation suggests that the synthesized heteroatom-doped CQDs exhibit a typical dependence on excitation wavelength, likely attributed to the surface defect states of the CQDs. The existence of various functional groups on their surfaces could generate multiple energy levels within the energy gap, resulting in the observed excitation-dependent behavior of the CQDs (Ji et al., 2018).

The XPS energy spectra of the synthesized heteroatom-doped CQDs are shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5A-F depict the full XPS spectra of the CQDs, showing that all the synthesized heteroatom-doped CQDs contain carbon (C) and oxygen (O). Table 1 displays the elemental composition of the CQDs that have been doped with various heteroatoms. The results indicate that N, B, K, Ca, and Se were successfully incorporated into the CQDs in this research.

A comprehensive analysis was conducted based on the C1s spectra from the XPS of the synthesized heteroatom-doped CQDs (Fig. 5G). The C1s energy peaks for N-CQDs are divided into three distinct peaks at 284.8, 286.51, and 288.27 eV, which correspond to C-C, C-O/C-N, and O-CO bonds, respectively (Zhan et al., 2018). The C1s peaks for B-CQDs are separated into five peaks at 284.08, 284.82, 286.33, 288.60, and 289.13 eV, representing C-B, C-C, C-O, O-CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Tian et al., 2017). The C1s spectra for Se-CQDs show a prominent peak at 284.8 eV, indicating the presence of sp2 carbon with a graphitic structure (Walekar et al., 2019), as well as a peak around 286.32 eV, suggesting the existence of C-Se, C-N, or C-O groups, while a peak near 288.0 eV is attributed to the CO group. The C1s spectra for K-CQDs are like those of Ca-CQDs, featuring peaks for C-C, C-O, and O-CO bonds.

The high-resolution O1s spectra for N-CQDs, K-CQDs, and Se-CQDs reveal two chemical states corresponding to C-O and CO, while the O1s spectra for Ca-CQDs and B-CQDs show a single chemical state related to C-O (Fig. 5H).

Fig. 5I illustrates the XPS energy spectra of the doped elements. The high-resolution N1s spectra for N-CQDs confirm the presence of three N species: pyridine N (398.80 eV), pyrrole N (401.32 eV), and amine N (400.42 eV) (Yadav et al., 2018). Pyridine-N and pyrrole-N are bonded to two carbon atoms in a hexagonal configuration, while amino N predominantly occurs in N-doped CQDs. The high-resolution Se3d spectra for Se-CQDs show significant Se3d signals, indicating successful incorporation of Se into the CQD structure through Se-O bond formation, which enhances the water solubility of Se-CQDs (Su et al., 2024). The Ca energy peaks for Ca-CQDs at 350.93 eV and 347.39 eV are split into two peaks corresponding to Ca2p3/2 and Ca2p1/2, while the K energy peaks for K-CQDs at 295.49 eV and 292.72 eV are also split into two peaks corresponding to K2p3/2 and K2p1/2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of antioxidant capacity of heteroatom-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant capacity of fruits and vegetables indicates their ability to combat free radicals and oxidative stress, typically evaluated using ABTS and DPPH methods. This study also investigated the free radical scavenging ability of heteroatom-doped CQDs at various concentrations using these two methods. The findings revealed that the scavenging rate increased with higher concentrations of the CQDs ( Fig. 6). Notably, the scavenging ability of all doped CQDs was greater in the ABTS assay (Fig. 6A) than in the DPPH assay (Fig. 6B), likely due to the better solubility and structural stability of CQDs in the aqueous ABTS solution compared to the methanolic DPPH solution (Ezati, Priyadarshi et al., 2022).

The antioxidant capacity of CQDs varies depending on the heteroatoms they are doped with. Among them, Ca-CQDs demonstrated superior free radical scavenging activity across all tested concentrations, which led us to select Ca-CQDs for further investigation into their anti-browning effects on fresh-cut potatoes. The enhanced antioxidant activity of Ca-CQDs may be linked to the role of their surface functional groups in scavenging free radicals and forming more stable products in the ABTS and DPPH tests (Zhang et al., 2018). Additionally, N-CQDs exhibited significant antioxidant activity, potentially due to the electronegativity difference between N and carbon (Caneschi et al., 2017). Se-CQDs also showed high antioxidant activity, likely due to the unique electronegativity of Se, which contributes to effective antioxidant defense and redox regulation (Dave & Gomes, 2019). Furthermore, K-CQDs displayed higher antioxidant activity than B-CQDs, which had the lowest activity. This difference may be attributed to B-CQDs generate additional OH radicals, which tend to recombine quickly to restore stability and prevent the quenching of oxidative radicals (Ezati, Roy et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Ca-CQDs treatments on browning of fresh-cut potatoes during storage period
ce:section.ce:para: The BI value indicates the extent of browning, with higher values reflecting greater browning (Arnold & Gramza-Michałowska, 2022). To identify the optimal concentration of Ca-CQDs for preventing browning in fresh-cut potatoes, we tested various concentrations of Ca-CQDs. The findings revealed that increased concentrations of Ca-CQDs resulted in reduced browning in fresh-cut potatoes stored in refrigeration for 8 d. Notably, there was no significant difference in BI values at concentrations of 1.00 mg/mL and 1.25 mg/mL ( Fig. 7A). Consequently, we selected 1.00 mg/mL of Ca-CQDs for subsequent experiments.

Additionally, the deionized water was employed as a control to compare the effects of Ca-CQDs, CQDs, and Ca2+ on the browning of fresh-cut potatoes during cold storage. The results indicated that all treatments—Ca-CQDs, CQDs, and Ca2+—significantly reduced the BI (Fig. 7B). Among these, Ca-CQDs demonstrated the most effective inhibition, outperforming all other treatments from the 6th d of cold storage, although its initial inhibitory effect was not significantly different from that of Ca2+ alone. These results suggest that Ca-CQDs have promising potential for preventing browning in fresh-cut fruits and vegetables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Ca-CQDs on browning-related enzymes, total phenol, MDA content and antioxidant capacity of fresh-cut potatoes
ce:section.ce:para: PPO, POD, PAL, and TYR are key enzymes involved in the enzymatic browning of fresh-cut fruits and vegetables. Specifically, PPO facilitates the oxidation of polyphenolic compounds, resulting in oxidation products that cause tissue browning; POD promotes the oxidation of phenolics using hydrogen peroxide, which also contributes to tissue browning; and TYR catalyzes the conversion of monophenols to σ-diphenols and their subsequent transformation into σ-quinones, which can polymerize and interact with intracellular proteins to form black or brown pigments (Glagoleva et al., 2020). In this research, treatments with Ca-CQDs and Ca2+ significantly decreased the PPO activity in freshly cut potato slices compared to the control (p < 0.05) ( Fig. 8A). Likewise, treatments with Ca-CQDs, Ca2+, and CQDs notably reduced the activities of POD (Fig. 8B) and TYR (Fig. 8C). Among these, Ca-CQDs exhibited a stronger inhibitory effect on POD and TYR during storage up to the 8th d, which helped minimize browning in fresh-cut potatoes. These findings indicate that CQDs, when doped with Ca, can effectively inhibit the activities of PPO, POD, and TYR, potentially explaining why Ca-CQDs are more effective at preventing browning in fresh-cut potatoes.

PAL is responsible for converting L-phenylalanine into trans-cinnamic acid, serving as an essential enzyme in the synthesis of fruit polyphenols (Luo et al., 2008; Nath et al., 2022). During the cold storage of fresh-cut potatoes, all treatments resulted in varying increases in PAL activity, with the most significant increases observed in the Ca2+ and Ca-CQDs treatments (Fig. 8D). Consequently, the total phenol content in fresh-cut potatoes tended to rise during storage, peaking in the Ca-CQDs and Ca2+ treatments (Fig. 8E), which may enhance the tissues' ability to scavenge free radicals. Ban et al. (2021) also reported that adding exogenous Ca increased the phenolic content and antioxidant capacity in aubergine (Solanum melongena L.).

MDA is the final product of membrane lipid peroxidation, and its levels indicate the degree of cell membrane damage (Hu et al., 2021). Lower MDA levels suggest a reduced likelihood of enzymatic browning. In this study, MDA levels were lower than the control across all treatments. Notably, the MDA levels in the Ca-CQDs and CQDs treatments were consistently lower than those in the other treatments starting from the 4th d of cold storage (Fig. 8F).

The free radical scavenging rate is a useful measure for assessing the antioxidant properties of fruits and vegetables. Enhancing the free radical scavenging ability of fresh-cut potatoes can help reduce browning in these potatoes (Kasnak, 2022). This research revealed that treatments with Ca2+, CQDs (except for the first 4 d regarding DPPH radical scavenging rate), and Ca-CQDs all improved DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging rates to different degrees compared to the control. Notably, the DPPH (Fig. 8G) and ABTS (Fig. 8H) free radical scavenging rates were consistently higher in the Ca-CQDs treatment than in the CQDs and Ca2+ treatments (p < 0.05), which may be a significant factor in the reduction of browning in fresh-cut potatoes following Ca-CQDs treatment.

In this experiment, N-, Ca-, Se-, B- and K-doped CQDs were synthesized separately, and the Ca-CQDs with the best antioxidant effects were identified by their in vitro scavenging rates of DPPH and ABTS. Then, the optimal concentrations of Ca-CQDs for inhibiting potato browning during cold storage and their effects on browning-related enzymes, total phenolics, MDA and total antioxidant capacity were further investigated. It was found that the application of Ca-CQDs at 1 mg/mL significantly reduced potato PPO, POD, TYR activities and MDA content, while increased PAL activity, total phenol content and scavenging ability of ABTS and DPPH radicals, thereby delaying the browning of freshly cut potatoes. This study can provide a reference for the application of Ca-doped CQDs materials in the storage and preservation of fresh-cut fruits and vegetables.

Yefei Gu: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Yanfang Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinhua Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Meng Lv: Methodology, Data curation. Jin Shang: Methodology, Investigation. Fujun Li: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiaoan Li: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [32272384; 32172278].
